questions.question: Explain the concept of framing in Data Link Layer. Describe any two methods of framing with examples.
keyPoints: Framing divides data stream into manageable frames with start/end markers
Character Stuffing: uses DLE STX (start) and DLE ETX (end) flags, escapes DLE in data
Bit Stuffing: inserts '0' after five consecutive '1's to prevent flag pattern (01111110) in data

questions.question: Compare Stop-and-Wait and Sliding Window protocols in terms of efficiency and implementation complexity.
keyPoints: Stop-and-Wait sends one frame at a time, waits for ACK - simple but inefficient
Sliding Window allows multiple frames in transit - efficient but complex
Stop-and-Wait channel utilization is low for high-speed networks
Sliding Window provides better throughput and channel utilization

questions.question: Describe the error detection and correction capabilities of Hamming Code with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Hamming Code detects and corrects single-bit errors
Requires r parity bits where 2^r >= m+r+1
Parity bits placed at positions 1,2,4,8... (powers of 2
Example: For 4-bit data (1011), 3 parity bits needed at positions 1,2,4

questions.question: Explain the working of Selective Repeat ARQ protocol. Why is it more efficient than Go-Back-N?
keyPoints: Selective Repeat retransmits only the corrupted/lost frame
Sender and receiver windows are equal (2^(n-1))
Out-of-order frames are buffered at receiver
More efficient because only failed frame is retransmitted, not subsequent frames

questions.question: What is CRC? Explain how it detects errors. Mention the standard CRC polynomials used.
keyPoints: CRC uses polynomial division to generate checksum
Sender divides data by generator polynomial, appends remainder
Receiver divides received data by same polynomial, if remainder is zero - no error
Standard polynomials: CRC-8, CRC-16, CRC-32 (Ethernet, ZIP, PNG)

questions.question: Explain the working of Go-Back-N ARQ protocol with the help of a diagram. Also derive the maximum window size for the protocol.
keyPoints: Go-Back-N uses cumulative ACK, sender has window size N
When frame k is lost, receiver discards frames k+1, k+2...
Sender times out and retransmits frames k, k+1, k+2...
Window size is 2^n - 1 where n is sequence number bits
If window is 2^n, ambiguity occurs between old and new frames
Diagram shows timeline: sender transmits frames 0-4, frame 2 lost, retransmits 2-4

questions.question: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the format of an HDLC frame. Describe the functions of each field.
keyPoints: HDLC Frame: Flag (8 bits) - Address (8/16 bits) - Control (8/16 bits) - Data (variable) - FCS (16/32 bits) - Flag
Flag (01111110): marks frame start and end
Address: identifies secondary station (broadcast if all 1s)
Control: identifies frame type (I-frame, S-frame, U-frame) and contains sequence numbers
Data: contains user information (0 or more bits)
FCS: error detection using CRC

questions.question: Describe the various flow control techniques used in Data Link Layer protocols. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Stop-and-Wait: simple, one frame at a time, low efficiency
Sliding Window: multiple frames, better throughput
XON/XOFF: software flow control using control characters
Hardware Flow Control: using RTS/CTS signals
Stop-and-Wait advantages: simple implementation, minimal memory
Sliding Window advantages: high efficiency, full duplex
Sliding Window disadvantages: complex, more memory for buffering

questions.question: Explain the difference between character stuffing and bit stuffing. Which one is used in HDLC and why?
keyPoints: Character stuffing: works with character-oriented protocols (Bisync), uses escape characters (DLE)
Bit stuffing: works with bit-oriented protocols (HDLC), inserts single '0' after five '1's
HDLC uses bit stuffing because it is more efficient (single bit vs character overhead)
Bit stuffing allows arbitrary bit patterns in data, more flexible
Character stuffing limited to character sets (typically ASCII), less efficient
